questions.text: For an ideal gas, the condition d Q = d W holds good in which of the following ?
questions.questionText: For an ideal gas, the condition d Q = d W holds good in which of the following ?
questions.question: For an ideal gas, the condition d Q = d W holds good in which of the following ?
questions.prompt: For an ideal gas, the condition d Q = d W holds good in which of the following ?
questions.html: For an ideal gas, the condition dQ=dW holds good in which of the following ?
questions.questionHtml: For an ideal gas, the condition dQ=dW holds good in which of the following ?
questions.solutionText: Solution From the first law of thermodynamics: d Q = d U + d W For d Q = d W , we need d U = 0 For an ideal gas, d U = n C v d T , so d U = 0 ⇒ d T = 0 Constant temperature ⇒ Isothermal process Answer: (2) Isothermal process
questions.solution: Solution From the first law of thermodynamics: d Q = d U + d W For d Q = d W , we need d U = 0 For an ideal gas, d U = n C v d T , so d U = 0 ⇒ d T = 0 Constant temperature ⇒ Isothermal process Answer: (2) Isothermal process
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
From the first law of thermodynamics: dQ=dU+dW

For dQ=dW, we need dU=0

For an ideal gas, dU=nCvdT, so dU=0⇒dT=0

Constant temperature ⇒ Isothermal process

Answer: (2) Isothermal process

questions.solutionText: Solution Energy of a photon, E = h c λ = 6.63 × 10 - 34 × 3 × 10 8 590 × 10 - 9 = 6.63 × 3 59 × 10 - 18 W Light energy produced per second = 90 100 × 10 = 9 W The required number of photons emitted per second will be the energy of one photon divided by the total light energy produced per second. This is given by, 9 × 59 6.63 × 3 × 10 - 18 = 2.67 × 10 19 = 0 . 267 × 10 20

questions.solutionText: Solution Assertion: The average thermal velocity of electrons in a conductor is zero. (True) Reason: Directions of motion of electrons are randomly oriented. (True) Since electrons move randomly in all directions, their velocity vectors cancel out on average, making the net average thermal velocity zero. The reason correctly and directly explains the assertion. Answer: (1) Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Answer: (1) Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

By conservation of momentum: M u 1 + M u 2 = 2 M v v = u 1 + u 2 2 Loss in KE = 1 2 M u 1 2 + 1 2 M u 2 2 − 1 2 ( 2 M ) v 2 = M 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − M ( u 1 + u 2 2 ) 2 = M 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − M ( u 1 + u 2 ) 2 4 = M 4 [ 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − ( u 1 + u 2 ) 2 ] = M 4 [ 2 u 1 2 + 2 u 2 2 − u 1 2 − 2 u 1 u 2 − u 2 2 ] = M 4 ( u 1 2 − 2 u 1 u 2 + u 2 2 ) = M 4 ( u 2 − u 1 ) 2 Answer: (3) M 4 ( u 2 − u 1 ) 2

By conservation of momentum: M u 1 + M u 2 = 2 M v v = u 1 + u 2 2 Loss in KE = 1 2 M u 1 2 + 1 2 M u 2 2 − 1 2 ( 2 M ) v 2 = M 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − M ( u 1 + u 2 2 ) 2 = M 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − M ( u 1 + u 2 ) 2 4 = M 4 [ 2 ( u 1 2 + u 2 2 ) − ( u 1 + u 2 ) 2 ] = M 4 [ 2 u 1 2 + 2 u 2 2 − u 1 2 − 2 u 1 u 2 − u 2 2 ] = M 4 ( u 1 2 − 2 u 1 u 2 + u 2 2 ) = M 4 ( u 2 − u 1 ) 2 Answer: (3) M 4 ( u 2 − u 1 ) 2

Solution
For perfectly inelastic collision, both masses stick together.

By conservation of momentum:
Mu1+Mu2=2Mv
v=u1+u22

=M2(u12+u22)−M(u1+u22)2

questions.solutionText: Solution We know, E = x · l ...(i) E = Emf of the cell x = Potential gradient l = Balancing length E = 6 mV x = I R P Q L ...(ii) Where, R P Q is the resistance of potentiometer wire and L is the length of potentiometer wire. I = E p ( R + R P Q ) ...(iii) where E P is the emf of the cell in primary circuit. So, from equations (i), (ii) and (iii), E = ( E p ( R + R P Q ) × R P Q L ) × l 6 × 10 - 3 = ( 2 ( R + 5 ) × 5 1 ) × ( 0 . 6 ) R = 995 Ω `

I = E p ( R + R P Q ) ...(iii) where E P is the emf of the cell in primary circuit. questions.solutionText: Solution Given: m = 5 kg, μ s = 0.6 , μ k = 0.4 , g = 10 m/s 2 Maximum static friction: f s = μ s m g = 0.6 × 5 × 10 = 30 N Kinetic friction: f k = μ k m g = 0.4 × 5 × 10 = 20 N Case 1: Applied force = 25 N < 30 N, so block does not move. Friction = 25 N (static, self-adjusting) Case 2: Applied force = 40 N > 30 N, so block moves. Friction = f k = 20 N Answer: B) 25 N and 20 N ^
questions.solution: Solution Given: m = 5 kg, μ s = 0.6 , μ k = 0.4 , g = 10 m/s 2 Maximum static friction: f s = μ s m g = 0.6 × 5 × 10 = 30 N Kinetic friction: f k = μ k m g = 0.4 × 5 × 10 = 20 N Case 1: Applied force = 25 N < 30 N, so block does not move. Friction = 25 N (static, self-adjusting) Case 2: Applied force = 40 N > 30 N, so block moves. Friction = f k = 20 N Answer: B) 25 N and 20 N ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given: m=5 kg, μs=0.6, μk=0.4, g=10 m/s2

Maximum static friction: fs=μsmg=0.6×5×10=30 N
Kinetic friction: fk=μkmg=0.4×5×10=20 N

Case 1: Applied force = 25 N < 30 N, so block does not move. Friction = 25 N (static, self-adjusting)

Case 2: Applied force = 40 N > 30 N, so block moves. Friction = fk = 20 N

Answer: B) 25 N and 20 N
^

questions.solutionText: Solution Ball hits one end of the rod and sticks to it. Use conservation of angular momentum about the centre of the rod. Centre of mass shifts to L 4 from the struck end (since m ⋅ L 2 + m ⋅ 0 2 m ... let centre of rod = origin, ball hits at + L 2 ): x c m = m ( 0 ) + m ( L 2 ) 2 m = L 4 from centre of rod I c m = m L 2 12 + m ( L 4 ) 2 + m ( L 2 − L 4 ) 2 = m L 2 12 + m L 2 16 + m L 2 16 = m L 2 12 + m L 2 8 = 2 m L 2 + 3 m L 2 24 = 5 m L 2 24 Angular momentum about x c m : L i = m v 0 ( L 2 − L 4 ) = m v 0 ⋅ L 4 Also, CM moves with v c m = m v 0 2 m = v 0 2 5 m L 2 24 ω = m v 0 ⋅ L 4 ω = m v 0 L / 4 5 m L 2 / 24 = v 0 4 × 24 5 L = 6 v 0 5 L Answer: (1) 6 v 0 5 L .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Ball hits one end of the rod and sticks to it. Use conservation of angular momentum about the centre of the rod.

Centre of mass shifts to L4 from the struck end (since m⋅L2+m⋅02m... let centre of rod = origin, ball hits at +L2):
xcm=m(0)+m(L2)2m=L4 from centre of rod

Icm=mL212+m(L4)2+m(L2−L4)2
=mL212+mL216+mL216
=mL212+mL28=2mL2+3mL224=5mL224

Angular momentum about xcm:
Li=mv0(L2−L4)=mv0⋅L4

Also, CM moves with vcm=mv02m=v02

5mL224ω=mv0⋅L4

ω=mv0L/45mL2/24=v04×245L=6v05L

Answer: (1) 6v05L

questions.solutionText: Solution The wire has total length l , so side of square = l 4 . The frame rotates about side A D . Mass of each side = m 4 . Perpendicular distances of each side's Center of Mass from axis A D : Side A D : 0 Side A B : l 8 Side D C : l 8 Side B C : l 4 On rotating 90 ° , each Center of Mass drops by a height equal to its perpendicular distance from the axis. W = m 4 ⋅ g ( 0 + l 8 + l 8 + l 4 ) W = m g 4 ⋅ l 2 = m g l 8 *

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The wire has total length l, so side of square =l4.

The frame rotates about side AD. Mass of each side =m4.

Perpendicular distances of each side's Center of Mass from axis AD:
Side AD: 0
Side AB: l8
Side DC: l8
Side BC: l4

On rotating 90°, each Center of Mass drops by a height equal to its perpendicular distance from the axis.

W=m4⋅g(0+l8+l8+l4)

W=mg4⋅l2=mgl8

questions.solutionText: Solution Label nodes: Bottom-left = L, Bottom-right = R Tracing the path P → L → R → Q through batteries only: P to L: moving from + to - terminal of 10V battery V L = V P − 10 L to R: moving from + to - terminal of 7V battery V R = V L − 7 = V P − 17 R to Q: moving from - to + terminal of 5V battery V Q = V R + 5 = V P − 12 ∴ V P − V Q = 12 V Answer: (3) 12 V

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Label nodes: Bottom-left = L, Bottom-right = R


Tracing the path P → L → R → Q through batteries only:

P to L: moving from + to - terminal of 10V battery
VL=VP−10

L to R: moving from + to - terminal of 7V battery

R to Q: moving from - to + terminal of 5V battery
VQ=VR+5=VP−12

∴VP−VQ=12 V

Answer: (3) 12 V

questions.solutionText: Solution Aufbau principle states that orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy. The correct filling order is: 1 s → 2 s → 2 p → 3 s → . . . Option (4): 1 s 2 , 2 s 2 , 2 p 6 follows this order correctly. Option (1): 1 s 2 , 3 s 2 , 4 s 2 skips 2 s and 2 p — violates Aufbau. Option (2): 1 s 2 , 2 p 3 , 3 p 2 skips 2 s — violates Aufbau. Option (3): 1 s 2 , 2 s 2 , 3 s 2 skips 2 p — violates Aufbau.

Cl2 oxidises I− to I2, which dissolves in CHCl3 giving a violet colouration.

2NaI+Cl2→2NaCl+I2

On adding excess Cl2, I2 is further oxidised to IO3− (colourless):

I2+5Cl2+6H2O→2HIO3+10HCl

Hence the violet colour disappears. This confirms the sodium halide is iodide (NaI).

questions.html: The formation of O2+[PtF6]−is basis for the formation of xenon fluorides. This is because
questions.questionHtml: The formation of O2+[PtF6]−is basis for the formation of xenon fluorides. This is because
questions.solutionText: Solution The formation of O 2 + [ P t F 6 ] − by Bartlett showed that P t F 6 is a strong enough oxidising agent to oxidise O 2 . Bartlett noted that the first ionisation energy of Xe (1170 kJ/mol) is comparable to that of O 2 (1165 kJ/mol). Since P t F 6 could oxidise O 2 to O 2 + , it should similarly be able to oxidise Xe to X e + , leading to the formation of X e + [ P t F 6 ] − and subsequently other xenon fluorides. Hence the correct reason is that O 2 and Xe have comparable ionisation energies.

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The formation of O2+[PtF6]− by Bartlett showed that PtF6 is a strong enough oxidising agent to oxidise O2.

Bartlett noted that the first ionisation energy of Xe (1170 kJ/mol) is comparable to that of O2 (1165 kJ/mol).

Since PtF6 could oxidise O2 to O2+, it should similarly be able to oxidise Xe to Xe+, leading to the formation of Xe+[PtF6]− and subsequently other xenon fluorides.

Hence the correct reason is that O2 and Xe have comparable ionisation energies.

questions.solutionText: Solution Key Idea: The magnitude of Δ ∝ c t (the orbital splitting energy) is decided by the nature of ligand Strong field ligand has highest Δ oct . The increasing field strength is as I − < Br − < Cl − < F − < OH − < H 2 O < C 2 O 4 2 − < NH 3 < en < NO 2 − < CN − The CN − is the strongest ligand among these, hence the magnitude of Δ oct will be maximum in [ Co ( CN ) 6 ] 3 − . Note : The magnitude of Δ oct is also decided by oxidation state of the metal ion. Thus, greater the ionic charge on the central metal ion, the greater the value of Δ oct . But here the oxidatinon state of Co metal ion is same in all complexes.

questions.solutionText: Solution The C − C l carbon bears H , C l , C H 3 , and is adjacent to the carbon bearing H 3 C , H , and the p -methoxyphenyl group. In aqueous acetone, ionisation occurs via S N 1 to give a carbocation stabilised by the electron-donating p - O C H 3 group on the adjacent aryl ring. The planar carbocation is then attacked by H 2 O from both faces ⇒ racemisation at the C − C l carbon. A: O H on same side as original C l ⇒ retention product B: O H on opposite side ⇒ inversion product Both enantiomers (A and B) are formed. The other chiral centre is unaffected. C involves a rearrangement (methyl migration) which does not occur here as the carbocation is already stabilised by resonance. Answer: (1) A and B

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The C−Cl carbon bears H, Cl, CH3, and is adjacent to the carbon bearing H3C, H, and the p-methoxyphenyl group.

In aqueous acetone, ionisation occurs via SN1 to give a carbocation stabilised by the electron-donating p-OCH3 group on the adjacent aryl ring.

The planar carbocation is then attacked by H2O from both faces ⇒ racemisation at the C−Cl carbon.

A: OH on same side as original Cl ⇒ retention product
B: OH on opposite side ⇒ inversion product

Both enantiomers (A and B) are formed. The other chiral centre is unaffected.

C involves a rearrangement (methyl migration) which does not occur here as the carbocation is already stabilised by resonance.

Answer: (1) A and B

questions.solutionText: Solution The De Broglie wavelength is λ = h p , where h = Planck's constant and p = momentum of the electron. Also, momentum p = m v and kinetic energy E K = 1 2 m v 2 From the above equation, we can relate momentum p and kinetic energy E K as, p = 2 m E K Now the ratio of wavelength of hydrogen atom when the atom jumps from third excited state to ground state viz ( n = 4 to n = 1 ) λ 1 λ 2 = h p 1 h p 2 = p 2 p 1 = E K 2 E K 1 Also, we know E K n = − 13.6 Z 2 n 2 Using equation (1), λ 1 λ 2 = 1 2 4 2 So, the ratio of the wavelength of ground state { n = 1 } to 3 rd excited state { n = 4 } is 1 4 .

Now the ratio of wavelength of hydrogen atom when the atom jumps from third excited state to ground state viz (n=4 to n=1)

So, the ratio of the wavelength of ground state {n=1} to 3rd excited state {n=4} is 14.

questions.solution: Solution Complex: [ C o ( N H 3 ) 4 C l ( N O 2 ) ] C l Central metal: Co Ligands: 4 × N H 3 (tetraammine), 1 × C l − (chlorido), 1 × N O 2 − (nitrito-N) Counter ion: C l − Oxidation state of Co: x + 0 ( 4 ) + ( − 1 ) + ( − 1 ) = 0 ⇒ x = + 3 Naming: ligands in alphabetical order — chlorido, nitrito-N, tetraammine ⇒ tetraamminechloridonitrito-N-cobalt(III) chloride Answer: (1) tetraamminechloridonitrito-N-cobalt(III) chloride
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Complex: [Co(NH3)4Cl(NO2)]Cl

Central metal: Co
Ligands: 4×NH3 (tetraammine), 1×Cl− (chlorido), 1×NO2− (nitrito-N)
Counter ion: Cl−

Oxidation state of Co:
x+0(4)+(−1)+(−1)=0⇒x=+3

Naming: ligands in alphabetical order — chlorido, nitrito-N, tetraammine

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following compounds is the most basic?
questions.solutionText: Solution (1) Pyrrole-like N (NH in 5-membered ring): lone pair in conjugation with ring ⇒ very weakly basic. (2) Aniline ( − N H 2 on benzene): lone pair delocalised into ring ⇒ weakly basic ( p K b ≈ 9.4 ). (3) Pyridine-like N in 5-membered ring (imine type): lone pair in s p 2 orbital, partially delocalised ⇒ weakly basic. (4) Saturated N in a 5-membered ring (pyrrolidine): lone pair in s p 3 orbital, no resonance deactivation ⇒ most basic ( p K b ≈ 2.7 ). Answer: (4) Pyrrolidine is the most basic.
questions.solution: Solution (1) Pyrrole-like N (NH in 5-membered ring): lone pair in conjugation with ring ⇒ very weakly basic. (2) Aniline ( − N H 2 on benzene): lone pair delocalised into ring ⇒ weakly basic ( p K b ≈ 9.4 ). (3) Pyridine-like N in 5-membered ring (imine type): lone pair in s p 2 orbital, partially delocalised ⇒ weakly basic. (4) Saturated N in a 5-membered ring (pyrrolidine): lone pair in s p 3 orbital, no resonance deactivation ⇒ most basic ( p K b ≈ 2.7 ). Answer: (4) Pyrrolidine is the most basic.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) Pyrrole-like N (NH in 5-membered ring): lone pair in conjugation with ring ⇒ very weakly basic.

(2) Aniline (−NH2 on benzene): lone pair delocalised into ring ⇒ weakly basic (pKb≈9.4).

(3) Pyridine-like N in 5-membered ring (imine type): lone pair in sp2 orbital, partially delocalised ⇒ weakly basic.

(4) Saturated N in a 5-membered ring (pyrrolidine): lone pair in sp3 orbital, no resonance deactivation ⇒ most basic (pKb≈2.7).

Answer: (4) Pyrrolidine is the most basic.

questions.solutionText: Solution Statement I: Last rank is 58, so total students = 58. Sufficient. Statement II: Jay is 10th from top and 49th from bottom. Total = 10 + 49 − 1 = 58 . Sufficient. questions.solutionText: Solution sin θ + cos θ = 1 2 sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ + 2 sin θ cos θ = 1 4 ⇒ sin 2 θ = - 3 4 Now: cos 4 θ = 1 - 2 sin 2 2 θ = 1 - 2 - 3 4 2 = 1 - 2 × 9 16 = - 1 8 And sin 6 θ = 3 sin 2 θ - 4 sin 3 2 θ = 3 - 4 sin 2 2 θ · sin 2 θ = 3 - 4 9 16 · - 3 4 ⇒ 3 4 × - 3 4 = - 9 16 So, 16 [ sin 2 θ + cos 4 θ + sin 6 θ ] 16 - 3 4 - 1 8 - 9 16 = - 23

Now:

And sin6θ=3sin2θ-4sin32θ

⇒34×-34=-916

So, 16[sin2θ+cos4θ+sin6θ]

16-34-18-916=-23

questions.solutionText: Solution f ( x ) = 4 x − x 2 + cos − 1 ⁡ ( x 2 − 1 ) + log ⁡ ( cos ⁡ x ) Domain conditions: 1) 4 x − x 2 : requires x ≠ 0 , so defined for all x ≠ 0 2) cos − 1 ⁡ ( x 2 − 1 ) : requires − 1 ≤ x 2 − 1 ≤ 1 ⇒ 0 ≤ x 2 ≤ 2 ⇒ 0 ≤ x ≤ 4 3) log ⁡ ( cos ⁡ x ) : requires cos ⁡ x > 0 ⇒ x ∈ ( − π 2 , π 2 ) Intersection of all three conditions within ( − π 2 , π 2 ) : From (2): x ∈ [ 0 , 4 ] From (3): x ∈ ( − π 2 , π 2 ) From (1): x ≠ 0 Intersection: x ∈ ( 0 , π 2 ) Answer: (2) ( 0 , π 2 )

Solution
f(x)=4x−x2+cos−1⁡(x2−1)+log⁡(cos⁡x)

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, cos π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos 4 π 7 + cos 5 π 7 + cos 6 π 7 + cos 7 π 7 = cos π 7 + cos 6 π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos 5 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos 4 π 7 + cos π = cos π 7 + cos π - π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos π - 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos π - 3 π 7 + cos π = cos π 7 - cos π 7 + cos 2 π 7 - cos 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 - cos 3 π 7 + cos π = cos π = - 1
questions.solution: Solution Given, cos π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos 4 π 7 + cos 5 π 7 + cos 6 π 7 + cos 7 π 7 = cos π 7 + cos 6 π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos 5 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos 4 π 7 + cos π = cos π 7 + cos π - π 7 + cos 2 π 7 + cos π - 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 + cos π - 3 π 7 + cos π = cos π 7 - cos π 7 + cos 2 π 7 - cos 2 π 7 + cos 3 π 7 - cos 3 π 7 + cos π = cos π = - 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given,
cosπ7+cos2π7+cos3π7+cos4π7+cos5π7+cos6π7+cos7π7
=cosπ7+cos6π7+cos2π7+cos5π7+cos3π7+cos4π7+cosπ
=cosπ7+cosπ-π7+cos2π7+cosπ-2π7+cos3π7+cosπ-3π7+cosπ
=cosπ7-cosπ7+cos2π7-cos2π7+cos3π7-cos3π7+cosπ
=cosπ=-1

questions.solutionText: Solution sin ⁡ 2 x = 4 cos ⁡ x 2 sin ⁡ x cos ⁡ x − 4 cos ⁡ x = 0 2 cos ⁡ x ( sin ⁡ x − 2 ) = 0 cos ⁡ x = 0 or sin ⁡ x = 2 (rejected, as | sin ⁡ x | ≤ 1 ) cos ⁡ x = 0 ⇒ x = 2 n π ± π 2 , n ∈ Z

Solution
sin⁡2x=4cos⁡x

2sin⁡xcos⁡x−4cos⁡x=0

2cos⁡x(sin⁡x−2)=0

cos⁡x=0 or sin⁡x=2 (rejected, as |sin⁡x|≤1)

cos⁡x=0⇒x=2nπ±π2,n∈Z

Solution
The eccentricity of the ellipse y2+4x2−12x+6y+14=0 is 32.


Given ellipse,


y2+4x2−12x+6y+14=04x2−12x+y2+6y+14=04(x2−3x+94)+y2+6y+9=−14+9+94(x−32)2+(y+3)2=4(x−32)21+(y+3)24=1


Here, a2=1,b2=4


∴e=1−a2b2e=1−14e=32

questions.solutionText: Solution Let the given expression is E = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 158 ° cos 98 ° sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 157 ° cos 97 ° As we know cos 180 ° - θ = - cos θ and cos 90 ° + θ = - sin θ , So E = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 180 ° - 22 ° cos ( 90 ° + 8 ° ) sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 180 ° - 23 ° cos ( 90 ° + 7 ° ) = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 22 ° sin 8 ° sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 23 ° sin 7 ° Now, using sin A + B = sin A cos B + cos A sin B , above expression becomes E = sin 22 ° + 8 ° sin 23 ° + 7 ° E = sin 30 ° sin 30 ° = 1
questions.solution: Solution Let the given expression is E = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 158 ° cos 98 ° sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 157 ° cos 97 ° As we know cos 180 ° - θ = - cos θ and cos 90 ° + θ = - sin θ , So E = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 180 ° - 22 ° cos ( 90 ° + 8 ° ) sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 180 ° - 23 ° cos ( 90 ° + 7 ° ) = sin 22 ° cos 8 ° + cos 22 ° sin 8 ° sin 23 ° cos 7 ° + cos 23 ° sin 7 ° Now, using sin A + B = sin A cos B + cos A sin B , above expression becomes E = sin 22 ° + 8 ° sin 23 ° + 7 ° E = sin 30 ° sin 30 ° = 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Let the given expression is

E=sin22°cos8°+cos158°cos98°sin23°cos7°+cos157°cos97°

As we know cos180°-θ=-cosθ and

cos90°+θ=-sinθ, So

E=sin22°cos8°+cos180°-22°cos(90°+8°)sin23°cos7°+cos180°-23°cos(90°+7°)

=sin22°cos8°+cos22°sin8°sin23°cos7°+cos23°sin7°

Now, using

sinA+B=sinAcosB+cosAsinB, above expression becomes

E=sin22°+8°sin23°+7°

E=sin30°sin30°=1

Solution
Matrix has no inverse when det=0.

|1−1x1x1x−11|=0

=1(x+1)+1(1−x)+x(−1−x2)

questions.solutionText: Solution The given function is f x = 1 + x , 0 ≤ x ≤ 2 3 - x , 2 < x ≤ 3 Let us first check continuity of f x at x = 2 . Now, LHL = lim x → 2 - f x = lim h → 0 1 + 2 - h = 3 and RHL = lim x → 2 + f x = lim h → 0 3 - 2 + h = 1 Thus, LHL ≠ RHL at x = 2 Hence, f x is discontinuous at x = 2 . So, f f x may be discontinuous when i 1 + x = 2 (when 0 ≤ x ≤ 2 ) or i i 3 - x = 2 (when 2 < x ≤ 3 ) ⇒ x = 1 is the common solution for the both. Hence, f f x is discontinuous at x = 1 and x = 2 .
questions.solution: Solution The given function is f x = 1 + x , 0 ≤ x ≤ 2 3 - x , 2 < x ≤ 3 Let us first check continuity of f x at x = 2 . Now, LHL = lim x → 2 - f x = lim h → 0 1 + 2 - h = 3 and RHL = lim x → 2 + f x = lim h → 0 3 - 2 + h = 1 Thus, LHL ≠ RHL at x = 2 Hence, f x is discontinuous at x = 2 . So, f f x may be discontinuous when i 1 + x = 2 (when 0 ≤ x ≤ 2 ) or i i 3 - x = 2 (when 2 < x ≤ 3 ) ⇒ x = 1 is the common solution for the both. Hence, f f x is discontinuous at x = 1 and x = 2 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The given function is fx=1+x,0≤x≤23-x,2<x≤3

Let us first check continuity of fx at x=2.

Now, LHL=limx→2-fx=limh→01+2-h=3

and RHL=limx→2+fx=limh→03-2+h=1

Thus, LHL≠RHL at x=2

Hence, fx is discontinuous at x=2.

So, ffx may be discontinuous when i 1+x=2 (when 0≤x≤2) or

ii 3-x=2 (when 2<x≤3)

⇒ x=1 is the common solution for the both.

Hence, ffx is discontinuous at x=1 and x=2.

2y=cot-13cosx+sinxcosx-3sinx2

=cot-13+tanx1-3tanx2

=cot-1tanπ3+x2

=cot-1cotπ2-π3+x 2
